eli3: Masked Language Modeling (MLM) is a pre-training objective where random tokens in a sentence are replaced with a [MASK] token, and the model must predict the original tokens. Unlike autoregressive LMs that predict left-to-right, MLM lets the model see the full sentence in both directions (except the masked positions), learning richer bidirectional representations.

BERT (Devlin et al. 2019) introduced MLM as its primary pre-training task. Strategy: randomly select 15% of tokens; replace 80% of those with [MASK], 10% with a random token, 10% keep unchanged. The 10%/10% trick prevents the model from only learning to predict [MASK] tokens — all tokens must be correctly represented. The encoder sees the full (partially masked) sentence, making representations bidirectional.

MLM produces better representations than autoregressive LM for understanding tasks (classification, NER, QA) because each token's representation benefits from both left and right context. The trade-off: MLM models cannot directly generate text (no left-to-right factorization). They excel as encoders: take the [CLS] token representation for classification, or token representations for token-level tasks (NER, SQuAD).

whenWhy.title: Pre-training encoders for understanding tasks
whenWhy.body: MLM pre-training produces powerful encoder representations. Fine-tuning protocol: take pre-trained BERT, add a task-specific head (linear classifier on [CLS] for classification, token-level classifier for NER), fine-tune on labeled data. This transfer learning approach achieves state-of-the-art on GLUE benchmark tasks (SST-2, MNLI, SQuAD) with relatively small labeled datasets. MLM encoders outperform autoregressive models on most NLU tasks due to bidirectional context.

whenWhy.title: Named entity recognition and span tasks
whenWhy.body: For token-level tasks (NER, POS tagging, coreference), bidirectional context is critical: whether 'Apple' refers to the company or fruit depends on surrounding words. BERT-style MLM produces a contextualized representation for each token that already incorporates full sentence context. Fine-tuning: add a linear layer on top of each token representation, train on labeled NER data. Outperforms LSTM-CRF and other sequence labeling models that use only left context.

whenWhy.title: Variants: RoBERTa, DeBERTa, ALBERT
whenWhy.body: MLM became the dominant pre-training paradigm for encoders with many variants. RoBERTa (Liu et al. 2019): larger batches, more data, no NSP, dynamic masking (re-mask each epoch) — significant gains over BERT. ALBERT (Lan et al. 2020): parameter sharing across layers + sentence-order prediction instead of NSP — 89× fewer parameters than BERT-large with similar performance. DeBERTa (He et al. 2021): disentangled attention for position and content separately — best encoder on many GLUE tasks.

deepDiveIntro: MLM objective: L_MLM = −Σ_{i ∈ M} log P(wᵢ | w_{[M]\i}; θ), where M is the set of masked positions and w_{[M]\i} is the sequence with all masked tokens replaced (by [MASK] or random). Only masked positions contribute to the loss — the model predicts the vocabulary distribution at each masked position using a linear head + softmax over vocabulary.

Architecture for MLM: transformer encoder (bidirectional self-attention). Unlike GPT (causal mask), BERT uses full attention — token i attends to all other tokens j ≠ i. This is why the representations are bidirectional. Pretraining scale: BERT-base was pre-trained on 16GB (BooksCorpus + Wikipedia), 1M steps, batch size 256. RoBERTa was pre-trained on 160GB, 500K steps, batch size 8192. Scale matters significantly for MLM quality.

deepDive.heading: Masking Strategy and Token Selection
deepDive.body: 15% token selection: chosen uniformly at random (original BERT) or using whole-word masking (SpanBERT, RoBERTa-WWM). Of selected: 80% → [MASK], 10% → random vocab token, 10% → unchanged. Rationale: the model cannot know which tokens are [MASK] vs. random vs. unchanged at test time (no [MASK] tokens in fine-tuning). This forces the model to build good representations for ALL tokens, not just [MASK] positions. Whole word masking (WWM): mask all subword tokens of a selected word — prevents easy reconstruction from partial word context. [1]
deepDive.formula: L = −(1/|M|) Σ_{i ∈ M} log P(wᵢ | x_{masked}; θ)

deepDive.heading: Span Masking Variants (SpanBERT, T5)
deepDive.body: SpanBERT (Joshi et al. 2020): mask contiguous spans rather than individual tokens. Span length sampled from geometric distribution (mean ≈ 3.8 tokens). Span Boundary Objective (SBO): predict each masked token using only the boundary token representations (first and last visible tokens on each side of span) — forces encoding of span-level information. Improves on coreference resolution and QA. T5 (Raffel et al. 2020): sentinel tokens replace masked spans — model must predict the full masked spans as a sequence (generative MLM). [1][2]

deepDive.heading: MLM vs. NSP and Sentence-Level Objectives
deepDive.body: BERT uses Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) alongside MLM: given (A, B) sentence pair, predict if B follows A in the original text (50% positive, 50% random). RoBERTa found NSP harmful — hurts MLM because NSP encourages shorter sequences. Sentence-Order Prediction (SOP, ALBERT): predict whether two sentences are in correct order (positive) or swapped (negative) — harder than NSP, more useful signal. ELECTRA: replaced token detection instead of MLM — discriminator classifies each token as real or generated by a generator network. More efficient than MLM (all tokens contribute, not just 15%). [1]

deepDive.heading: Fine-tuning BERT for Downstream Tasks
deepDive.body: Classification: input [CLS] + sentence → [CLS] token representation h_[CLS] → linear + softmax → label. Pair tasks (NLI, STS): input [CLS] + sentence A + [SEP] + sentence B + [SEP] → classify. Token classification (NER, POS): all token representations hᵢ → linear + softmax → per-token label. Span extraction (SQuAD QA): predict start/end positions of answer span — dot product of each token representation with learned start/end vectors. Fine-tuning hyperparameters: learning rate 2e-5 to 5e-5, batch size 16–32, 2–4 epochs. BERT fine-tuning is sensitive to learning rate — use linear warmup + decay. [1]

takeaways: MLM: mask 15% tokens (80% [MASK], 10% random, 10% original); train to predict originals — bidirectional encoder. [1]
MLM produces richer representations than causal LM for NLU tasks; cannot directly generate text. [1]
Whole-word masking and span masking improve over token-level masking for span-level tasks. [1]
RoBERTa: more data, larger batches, no NSP, dynamic masking — significantly outperforms BERT. [1]
ELECTRA: replaced token detection is more efficient than MLM (all tokens contribute to loss). [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does BERT use 80/10/10 masking rather than replacing all selected tokens with [MASK]?
edgeCases.a: If 100% of selected tokens were [MASK]: (1) During fine-tuning, the model never sees [MASK] tokens (they don't appear in real text) — train/test mismatch. The model's representations for non-masked tokens might be poorly calibrated. (2) The model could learn to treat [MASK] as a special token with no effort on other tokens. The 10% random token forces the model to build good representations for every token in the vocabulary (since any token could be randomly replaced). The 10% unchanged trains the model to predict the correct token even when nothing looks masked. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is ELECTRA's replaced token detection and why is it more efficient than MLM?
edgeCases.a: ELECTRA (Clark et al. 2020): two-model setup. Generator (small MLM): predicts masked tokens (standard MLM). Discriminator (same-size or larger): for each token, binary classify whether it was replaced by the generator or is original. Key insight: all tokens contribute to the discriminator's loss (not just 15% as in MLM) — 15.3× more efficient signal per training step. Generator is trained to produce plausible (not obviously wrong) replacements — harder for discriminator. After pre-training, only the discriminator is fine-tuned for downstream tasks. ELECTRA-large matches RoBERTa performance with 1/4 the compute. [1]

sources.label: Devlin et al. (2019) — BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, NAACL 2019
Raffel et al. (2020) — Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (T5), JMLR 2020
